old_claim.note: Internet Protocols (TCP/IP, ARPANET) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Full Internet Service in January 1983 required hosts and services to complete the NCP-to-IP/TCP transition, making the Internet protocol suite constitutive of this milestone.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction, General Plan, and Milestones (pp. 1-5) define the ARPA Internet, require complete NCP-to-IP/TCP conversion by 1 January 1983, and define Full Internet Service as all hosts and services being TCP-based.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RFC 801 makes completed IP/TCP deployment the explicit condition for the January 1983 Full Internet Service milestone.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The node is dated to that transition event and defined by TCP/IP, so the protocol suite is constitutive rather than one optional enabling route.

ontology_before: The Internet protocol suite was modeled as merely one enabling path to a node whose own definition and date are TCP/IP-specific.
ontology_after: The protocol lineage is a required direct prerequisite for the operational January 1983 TCP/IP Internet.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-internet-operational-boundary: internet_protocols_tcp_ip_arpanet remains a required Internet edge.
would_reject_if: The Internet node is broadened to pre-TCP experimental internetworking rather than the January 1983 Full Internet Service milestone.
short_reason: RFC 801 defines completed TCP/IP conversion as the condition for Full Internet Service, so the protocol edge is required.
